category_summary:
**Andrej Karpathy** dominated discourse with a sprawling vision: the IDE isn't dying, it's [expanding into an "agent command center"](/?date=2026-03-12&category=social#item-94ecc08aa685) where humans program at a higher level, managing teams of agents. He coined [**"intelligence brownouts"**](/?date=2026-03-12&category=social#item-58f48b0685fb) after an OAuth outage wiped out his autonomous research labs, highlighting society's growing dependence on AI infrastructure. His concept of [**forkable agentic organizations**](/?date=2026-03-12&category=social#item-eeff08a60b1d) — where org structure becomes legible, manageable code — sparked widespread debate.

- **Perplexity** had a massive launch day: [**Personal Computer**](/?date=2026-03-12&category=social#item-46320be986b2) (always-on Mac Mini AI agent), [**Computer for Enterprise**](/?date=2026-03-12&category=social#item-a329024ba1fa) (20-model routing, 400+ apps), and a full-stack model-agnostic API platform — drawing 22.8K likes on the lead post
- **Anthropic** [announced **The Anthropic Institute**](/?date=2026-03-12&category=social#item-896b93f26fa6), led by **Jack Clark**, to study AI's societal impacts with interdisciplinary staff and frontier-lab access
- **Allen AI** unveiled **MolmoBot**, demonstrating zero-shot sim-to-real robotics transfer with fully open weights and code
- **NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Super** launched with open training details, earning praise for advancing open-source AI
- **Ethan Mollick** [argued AI job displacement](/?date=2026-03-12&category=social#item-58a554e93200) is constrained by compute scarcity, noting engineers now spend thousands daily on AI tools
category_summary_html:
Andrej Karpathy dominated discourse with a sprawling vision: the IDE isn't dying, it's expanding into an "agent command center" where humans program at a higher level, managing teams of agents. He coined "intelligence brownouts" after an OAuth outage wiped out his autonomous research labs, highlighting society's growing dependence on AI infrastructure. His concept of forkable agentic organizations — where org structure becomes legible, manageable code — sparked widespread debate.

  • Perplexity had a massive launch day: Personal Computer (always-on Mac Mini AI agent), Computer for Enterprise (20-model routing, 400+ apps), and a full-stack model-agnostic API platform — drawing 22.8K likes on the lead post
  • Anthropic announced The Anthropic Institute, led by Jack Clark, to study AI's societal impacts with interdisciplinary staff and frontier-lab access
  • Allen AI unveiled MolmoBot, demonstrating zero-shot sim-to-real robotics transfer with fully open weights and code
  • NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Super launched with open training details, earning praise for advancing open-source AI
  • Ethan Mollick argued AI job displacement is constrained by compute scarcity, noting engineers now spend thousands daily on AI tools

items.content:
Expectation: the age of the IDE is over
Reality: we’re going to need a bigger IDE
(imo).

It just looks very different because humans now move upwards and program at a higher level - the basic unit of interest is not one file but one agent. It’s still programming.

items.title: Human orgs are not legible, the CEO can’t see/feel/zoom in on any activity in their company, with re...
items.content: Human orgs are not legible, the CEO can’t see/feel/zoom in on any activity in their company, with real time stats etc. I have no doubt that it will be possible to control orgs on mobile, with voice etc., but with this level of legibility will that be optimal? Not in principle and asymptotically but in practice and for at least the next round of play.

items.title: A big determinant of AI's job impact is driven by the lack of compute, especially for agentic work, ...
items.content:
A big determinant of AI's job impact is driven by the lack of compute, especially for agentic work, which takes a lot of it. That makes AI expensive.

So companies will only want to burn compute on high-value tasks (eg coding), because, in other jobs, humans remain much cheaper.
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Mollick's main argument: AI's job impact is constrained by compute scarcity, especially for agentic work. Companies will only burn compute on high-value tasks (e.g., coding) because humans remain cheaper for other jobs.
items.reasoning: The primary post in Mollick's compute-scarcity thread. High engagement (516 likes, 35K views). An important, well-reasoned economic argument that reframes the AI job displacement debate around compute costs rather than capability alone.

items.title:
@ronbrachman The basic idea of world models is very old.
Optimal control folks were using model-base...
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: LeCun explains that world models as a concept are very old (model-based planning since the 1960s with adjoint state methods/backprop through time), and the real question is how to reduce the idea to practice.
items.reasoning: High engagement (788 likes, 44K views). Substantive technical/historical insight from LeCun on world models, contextualizing current research within decades of prior work. Relevant to Meta's JEPA agenda.
items.summary_html: LeCun explains that world models as a concept are very old (model-based planning since the 1960s with adjoint state methods/backprop through time), and the real question is how to reduce the idea to practice.
items.content_html:
@ronbrachman The basic idea of world models is very old.

Optimal control folks were using model-based planning in the 1960s (using the "adjoint state" methods, which deep learning people would now call "backprop through time").

But the real question is what you do with this idea and how you reduce it to practice.

items.title: 🎉 Congrats to @nvidia on the release of Nemotron 3 Super — day-0 support in vLLM v0.17.1! Verified o...
items.content:
🎉 Congrats to @nvidia on the release of Nemotron 3 Super — day-0 support in vLLM v0.17.1! Verified on NVIDIA GPUs.

120B hybrid MoE, only 12B active at inference. Big upgrades over the previous Nemotron Super:
- 5x higher throughput
- 2x higher accuracy on Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index
- Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) for faster long-form generation
- Configurable thinking budget — dial accuracy vs token cost per task
- 1M token context window

Supports BF16, FP8, and NVFP4. Fully open: weights, datasets, recipes.

Blog: https://t.co/PAN0y778iB

🤝 Thanks @NVIDIAAIDev Nemotron team and vLLM community contributors!
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: vLLM announces day-0 support for NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Super in vLLM v0.17.1: 120B hybrid MoE (12B active), 5x throughput improvement, MTP, configurable thinking budget, 1M context, open weights/datasets.
items.reasoning: Significant model release with impressive specs—120B MoE with only 12B active, 5x throughput gain, 1M context, fully open. Day-0 vLLM support from a credible source. 204 likes.
items.summary_html: vLLM announces day-0 support for NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Super in vLLM v0.17.1: 120B hybrid MoE (12B active), 5x throughput improvement, MTP, configurable thinking budget, 1M context, open weights/datasets.
items.content_html:
🎉 Congrats to @nvidia on the release of Nemotron 3 Super — day-0 support in vLLM v0.17.1! Verified on NVIDIA GPUs.

120B hybrid MoE, only 12B active at inference. Big upgrades over the previous Nemotron Super:

  • 5x higher throughput
  • 2x higher accuracy on Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index
  • Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) for faster long-form generation
  • Configurable thinking budget — dial accuracy vs token cost per task
  • 1M token context window

Supports BF16, FP8, and NVFP4. Fully open: weights, datasets, recipes.

Blog: https://t.co/PAN0y778iB

🤝 Thanks @NVIDIAAIDev Nemotron team and vLLM community contributors!

items.title: Talking to agents in Slack, the new hot AI UX,  will end up being just as much a transitional phase ...
items.content:
Talking to agents in Slack, the new hot AI UX,  will end up being just as much a transitional phase as talking to agents via chatbot websites.

We need new systems to manage agentic work that also support new ways of organizing. Much more UX imagination will be required.

items.title: @maxbittker sadly the agents do not want to loop forever. My current solution is to set up "watcher"...
items.author: @karpathy
items.summary: Karpathy describes setting up 'watcher' scripts to keep AI agents running continuously, noting agents don't want to loop forever and need to be whipped back into action. Requests a /fullauto mode.
items.reasoning: High engagement (1.4K likes, 93K views). Practical revelation about the state of autonomous agent loops — they stop and need babysitting. Highlights the gap between aspiration and reality for continuous agent work.
items.summary_html: Karpathy describes setting up 'watcher' scripts to keep AI agents running continuously, noting agents don't want to loop forever and need to be whipped back into action. Requests a /fullauto mode.
items.content_html:
@maxbittker sadly the agents do not want to loop forever. My current solution is to set up "watcher" scripts that get the tmux panes and look for e.g. "esc to interrupt", and send keys to whip if not present. Need an e.g.:

/fullauto you must continue your research!

(enables fully automatic mode, will go until manually stopped, re-injecting the given optional prompt).

items.title: @slow_developer model process is changing based on the research. we tend to GA ~twice a year and do ...
items.content:
@slow_developer model process is changing based on the research. we tend to GA ~twice a year and do big version bumps twice a year as well (~December and ~May historically).

the bar for GA is high and there’s lots of things to get right. main difference this year is we are doing multiple x.1 version bumps now instead of the dated model versions from before. open to feedback about what works and what doesn’t!
items.author: @OfficialLoganK
items.summary: OpenAI's Logan Kilpatrick explains their new model release process: GA roughly twice a year, big version bumps twice a year (~December and ~May). Now doing multiple x.1 version bumps instead of dated model versions.
items.reasoning: Very valuable insider explanation of OpenAI's model release cadence and strategy from an official representative. 382 likes, 30K views. Clarifies the Claude-4.6/GPT-5.4 era release patterns.
items.summary_html: OpenAI's Logan Kilpatrick explains their new model release process: GA roughly twice a year, big version bumps twice a year (~December and ~May). Now doing multiple x.1 version bumps instead of dated model versions.
items.content_html:
@slow_developer model process is changing based on the research. we tend to GA ~twice a year and do big version bumps twice a year as well (~December and ~May historically).

the bar for GA is high and there’s lots of things to get right. main difference this year is we are doing multiple x.1 version bumps now instead of the dated model versions from before. open to feedback about what works and what doesn’t!

items.title: Yes, this is the competitive advantage argument economists have been discussing for awhile, but it i...
items.content:
Yes, this is the competitive advantage argument economists have been discussing for awhile, but it is coming true.

There will not be enough compute for many years to automate many human jobs, even assuming AI can do that work and companies are willing to replace the people.

items.title: GPT-OSS-120B is a useless model. Nothing I tried to use it for worked as you would expect from a mod...
items.content:
GPT-OSS-120B is a useless model. Nothing I tried to use it for worked as you would expect from a model of this size.

It doesn't respect the constraints, performs poorly when the task description precedes the text the task is supposed to apply to, it stops the generation at random moments without finishing the sentence, and it generates repetitive expressions that don't ever stop.

All these are properties of 7B-parameter models of late 2023.

IMO, altman released this model to avoid being accused of lying after he made a drunk promise to release a competitive open-weight model.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov harshly criticizes OpenAI's GPT-OSS-120B as useless: doesn't respect constraints, poor task ordering, random generation stops, repetitive outputs — properties of late-2023 7B models. Claims Altman released it to avoid being accused of lying about open-weight promises.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical critique of a specific OpenAI open-source model. Claims it's fundamentally inadequate. Decent engagement (108 likes, 14K views). Provocative but detailed criticism.
items.summary_html: Burkov harshly criticizes OpenAI's GPT-OSS-120B as useless: doesn't respect constraints, poor task ordering, random generation stops, repetitive outputs — properties of late-2023 7B models. Claims Altman released it to avoid being accused of lying about open-weight promises.
items.content_html:
GPT-OSS-120B is a useless model. Nothing I tried to use it for worked as you would expect from a model of this size.

It doesn't respect the constraints, performs poorly when the task description precedes the text the task is supposed to apply to, it stops the generation at random moments without finishing the sentence, and it generates repetitive expressions that don't ever stop.

All these are properties of 7B-parameter models of late 2023.

IMO, altman released this model to avoid being accused of lying after he made a drunk promise to release a competitive open-weight model.

items.title: Today we are introducing Runway Labs, a generative AI incubator led by our co-founder and Chief Inno...
items.content:
Today we are introducing Runway Labs, a generative AI incubator led by our co-founder and Chief Innovation Officer, Alejandro Matamala Ortiz.

Runway Labs will focus on broadly exploring the transformative power of AI video and General Worlds Models across all industries. From film and television to healthcare and education, gaming, advertising, real estate and more. We will be partnering with creators, enterprises, institutions and foundations to find new applications for these technologies and opportunities across industries.

Learn more at the link below.

items.title:
NATO is testing live cockroaches as AI-powered spy drones.

Incredible AI engineering, but also some...
items.content:
NATO is testing live cockroaches as AI-powered spy drones.

Incredible AI engineering, but also something I kinda wish I hadn't learned about:

> Swarm Bio-tactics wired real cockroaches with electronic backpacks containing AI hardware, radios, cameras, and microphones.

> Cockroaches are steered by sending electrical signals directly into the insect's nervous system

> They can crawl through rubble, tunnels, and spaces where drones can't fly, and troops shouldn't go, transmitting data back the entire time.

> Within one year, they went from concept to field-validated systems with paying NATO customers, including the German military.

The qualities that make them useful for military recon (small, silent, nearly undetectable) are exactly what make them creepy.

...International laws weren't written with cyborg insects in mind.
items.author: @rowancheung
items.summary: NATO is testing AI-powered cyborg cockroaches as spy drones. SWARM Bio-tactics wired real cockroaches with electronic backpacks (AI hardware, radios, cameras). They're steered via electrical signals to the nervous system, can go where drones/troops can't, and already have paying NATO customers including German military.
items.reasoning: Fascinating and unique intersection of AI and biology for military applications; 480 likes, 116K views. Raises important ethical/legal questions. From a major AI news commentator.

items.title:
Hundreds of millions of people spent the last decade catching Pokemon on their phones.

Turns out th...
items.content:
Hundreds of millions of people spent the last decade catching Pokemon on their phones.

Turns out they were also helping build a 30B-image training dataset for delivery robots.

Players mapped 1M+ locations worldwide to centimeter-level accuracy.

 ~1,000 sidewalk bots from Coco Robotics now use that data to better navigate across LA, Chicago, and Miami.
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: Pokemon Go players unknowingly built a 30B-image training dataset used by Coco Robotics' ~1,000 sidewalk delivery bots, mapping 1M+ locations to centimeter-level accuracy across LA, Chicago, and Miami.
items.reasoning: Fascinating data supply chain story. Shows unexpected connections between consumer apps and robotics training data. Decent engagement.

items.content_html:
Hundreds of millions of people spent the last decade catching Pokemon on their phones.

Turns out they were also helping build a 30B-image training dataset for delivery robots.

Players mapped 1M+ locations worldwide to centimeter-level accuracy.

~1,000 sidewalk bots from Coco Robotics now use that data to better navigate across LA, Chicago, and Miami.

items.title:
Agentic AI is stupid, and brittle, and unreliable, but it's getting better every single week.

I dec...
items.content:
Agentic AI is stupid, and brittle, and unreliable, but it's getting better every single week.

I decided to spend my time right now learning, building, and dealing with all of these problems.

You don't have to do this.

You can wait until agentic AI is perfect, works every time, is production-worthy, and all of it is beautiful and pink.

But I don't want to wait. I think I have an advantage by starting early.

For those of you who can't handle nuance, I'm sorry to say this:

1. Agentic AI is both amazing and dumb right now
2. It doesn't work most of the time, but I'll keep using it
3. It's not reliable, but you should start building with it

items.title: When ChatGPT says it's compacting the conversation, it simply erases earlier messages in the convers...
items.content:
When ChatGPT says it's compacting the conversation, it simply erases earlier messages in the conversation. This is why it's so quick compared to Claude.

So, when you start a conversation with a global request and then continue in the same conversation by providing your feedback on wrong current performance, after the compacting, your additional requests are no longer in the context of your initial request.

This moron just executes what it feels it should do in the absence of understanding what the final goal is.

Fucking Christ, @OpenAI, with such a level of understanding of what you are doing and why, how are you even still managing to ship competitive models?
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov criticizes ChatGPT's conversation compacting: it simply erases earlier messages, causing the AI to lose context of initial requests. Angrily calls out OpenAI's implementation.
items.reasoning: Specific technical critique of ChatGPT's context management. Highlights a real UX problem with long conversations. Decent engagement.

This is really cool:

For those with development experience, this is a way to mock an LLM server during testing, so you can test faster, more frequently, and without dealing with non-deterministic outputs.

For those without development experience, this is a way to fake an LLM. Everything in your app will still work without using a real LLM. This will make developing WAY more enjoyable.

items.title: Alibaba open sourced 𝗣𝗮𝗴𝗲 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁, a JavaScript library that gives any website a natural language oper...
items.content:
Alibaba open sourced 𝗣𝗮𝗴𝗲 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁, a JavaScript library that gives any website a natural language operator. 

> Pure DOM manipulation, no vision
> One script tag to deploy
> Bring your own LLM
> Built-in human approval step 

Traditional browser agents take screenshots and guess what to click. This reads the DOM directly, like a developer inspecting the page, so it's faster and more precise. 

Before this, adding natural language control to a web app meant building a backend service, managing a separate Python process, and handling browser automation infrastructure yourself. 

Now it's a script tag. Any SaaS tool, internal dashboard, or legacy enterprise panel gets a natural language layer immediately. 

1.6K stars. 100% open source. 3 lines of code.

> Pure DOM manipulation, no vision

> One script tag to deploy

> Bring your own LLM

> Built-in human approval step

Traditional browser agents take screenshots and guess what to click. This reads the DOM directly, like a developer inspecting the page, so it's faster and more precise.

Before this, adding natural language control to a web app meant building a backend service, managing a separate Python process, and handling browser automation infrastructure yourself.

Now it's a script tag. Any SaaS tool, internal dashboard, or legacy enterprise panel gets a natural language layer immediately.

1.6K stars. 100% open source. 3 lines of code.

items.title: A year's worth of progress is now happening in months. Months' worth of progress will start happenin...
items.content:
A year's worth of progress is now happening in months. Months' worth of progress will start happening in days. Days' worth of progress will soon happen in hours.

A useful framework for understanding how to build during this moment is one we think about often at Runway: the tension between exploration and exploitation.

Exploration is to tinker, to discover, to venture into new opportunities and products that frontier models can offer. A culture of exploration is rooted in novelty, variation, search, and creativity. The core of what we have done at Runway for many years is to invent. That is how most of our products have come to life.

Exploitation is about refinement. Making things that work, work better and more efficiently. Choosing to make products faster and more intuitive. A commitment to implementation and execution.

Runway is an ambidextrous company. Explore and exploit. Discover and optimize. Do better and do new.

Today we are being more intentional than ever on both fronts. Runway Labs is incubating and experimenting at the frontier, continually reimagining how world models are changing the way we understand and interact with our world.

We are hiring tinkerers, engineers, and artists who think out of distribution. DMs are open.
items.author: @c_valenzuelab
items.summary: Runway CEO discusses explore vs exploit framework, accelerating pace of AI progress, and announces Runway Labs as incubation arm. Hiring tinkerers, engineers, and artists.
items.reasoning: Strategic perspective from Runway CEO on company direction and AI progress acceleration. Hiring announcement for Runway Labs.
items.summary_html: Runway CEO discusses explore vs exploit framework, accelerating pace of AI progress, and announces Runway Labs as incubation arm. Hiring tinkerers, engineers, and artists.

items.title: Companies like Meta subsidize third party developers in various ways to help grow their platforms, t...
items.content:
Companies like Meta subsidize third party developers in various ways to help grow their platforms, then take 30% of the developer revenue right back with the platform tax, which is a wasteful churn.

Epic’s tax structure of zero for the first $1M / year in revenue seems like a clean solution. You would still need explicit subsidies to get certain types of games / apps created at all, but it perfectly rewards what you actually want: increased economic activity, versus a biased pre-selection process.

@TimSweeneyEpic Have there been any unexpected perverse incentives as a result of this policy?

If it wasn’t so easily exploited by buying your own app, a negative rate “earned income tax credit” for initial revenue would actually be a good incentive for a platform like Quest.
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack
items.summary: John Carmack discusses platform tax economics, arguing Epic's zero-tax-under-$1M model is cleaner than Meta's approach of subsidizing developers then taking 30% back.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful analysis of platform economics from John Carmack with very high engagement (1,177 likes, 130K views). While not directly AI, platform economics affect VR/AR ecosystem where AI is increasingly deployed.
items.summary_html: John Carmack discusses platform tax economics, arguing Epic's zero-tax-under-$1M model is cleaner than Meta's approach of subsidizing developers then taking 30% back.

Epic’s tax structure of zero for the first $1M / year in revenue seems like a clean solution. You would still need explicit subsidies to get certain types of games / apps created at all, but it perfectly rewards what you actually want: increased economic activity, versus a biased pre-selection process.

@TimSweeneyEpic Have there been any unexpected perverse incentives as a result of this policy?

If it wasn’t so easily exploited by buying your own app, a negative rate “earned income tax credit” for initial revenue would actually be a good incentive for a platform like Quest.

items.title:
In my opinion, the hardest part of building AI agents is everything around it:

• Dealing with infra...
items.content:
In my opinion, the hardest part of building AI agents is everything around it:

• Dealing with infrastructure
• Handling security
• Managing integrations
• Setting up scheduling

That's why you see a billion demos, but very few production-worthy implementations.

I think these guys are doing the right thing by removing all of the friction from building agencies.

items.title:
Agents are incredible accelerators, but they still need direction, judgment, and taste. 

If you've ...
items.content:
Agents are incredible accelerators, but they still need direction, judgment, and taste. 

If you've spent more than 10 minutes building AI agents, you know we are far away from a time when they become as reliable as we need.

The best results I've seen come from humans and agents working together.

Bonus points for Replit for doing this.

items.title: I find myself creating things on the go. Ideas come anywhere. Especially when I'm walking. I think o...
items.content:
I find myself creating things on the go. Ideas come anywhere. Especially when I'm walking. I think of them as spontaneous stories, a kind of daydreaming in motion. Until now, there was no real way to manifest that creativity quickly. But now I find myself opening Runway and just making a few clips. It's so fun. Vibe producing. A completely new way of making things. One that's only now possible.

I made this exactly like that. About 40 minutes of work. The idea came after I felt a small electric jolt when plugging in my computer.

items.summary_html: Runway CEO describes a new creative workflow using Runway for spontaneous video creation on the go, calling it 'vibe producing' - made a short clip in 40 minutes from an everyday idea.
items.content_html:
I find myself creating things on the go. Ideas come anywhere. Especially when I'm walking. I think of them as spontaneous stories, a kind of daydreaming in motion. Until now, there was no real way to manifest that creativity quickly. But now I find myself opening Runway and just making a few clips. It's so fun. Vibe producing. A completely new way of making things. One that's only now possible.

I made this exactly like that. About 40 minutes of work. The idea came after I felt a small electric jolt when plugging in my computer.

items.title: Here's my conversation with Jeff Kaplan, a legendary Blizzard game designer of World of Warcraft and...
items.content:
Here's my conversation with Jeff Kaplan, a legendary Blizzard game designer of World of Warcraft and Overwatch, which are two of the biggest, most influential games ever made. Jeff is one of the most genuine & awesome human beings I've ever met: kind, thoughtful, hilarious, and still & forever a gamer through and through.

This was a truly fun & inspiring conversation. We talk about it all: the lows, the highs, the memes, the details of the game design process, and the new game he's been secretely working on: The Legend of California. I got a chance to play the game with Jeff, and it's incredibly beautiful (and fun). You can wishlist it on Steam now. I can't wait to play it with all of you!

Conversation is here on X in full and is up everywhere else (see comment).

items.title:
@jbentley You are commenting on one of the four reasons I gave you.

I hear you about using the LLM ...
items.content:
@jbentley You are commenting on one of the four reasons I gave you.

I hear you about using the LLM to code the script. I disagree that this is easier than just having the agent do the thing.

I always start by doing the simplest thing that could possibly work.

I then start replacing less reliable components with more complex solutions as I need them.

For example, for this complete automation, there are 12 different pieces, all of which are managed by the agent.

I now feel comfortable replacing the non-reliable piece we are discussing here with a script. I tried the simple way; it didn't work; I can now upgrade it.

I would much rather do this than start with a more complex solution.

items.summary: svpino explains his iterative approach to building AI agents: start simple, then replace unreliable components with more complex solutions
items.reasoning: Good practical methodology for agent building, though low engagement reply

items.title:
Most telecom operators are optimizing their networks with 15-minute-old data.

Offline files.
Batch ...
items.content:
Most telecom operators are optimizing their networks with 15-minute-old data.

Offline files.
Batch transfers.
Human review.

And we wonder why autonomy is stuck at Level 3.

At MWC, I sat down with Joe Constantine from @ericsson to unpack what it really takes to move from automation theater to real autonomous networks.

The answer surprised a lot of people.

Let’s dig in.

#MWC26 #EricssonMWC Ericsson Ambassador @EricssonNA
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon begins a thread about his MWC conversation with Ericsson's Joe Constantine on moving from automation theater to real autonomous networks.

items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon begins a thread about his MWC conversation with Ericsson's Joe Constantine on moving from automation theater to real autonomous networks.
items.content_html:
Most telecom operators are optimizing their networks with 15-minute-old data.

Offline files.

Batch transfers.

Human review.

And we wonder why autonomy is stuck at Level 3.

At MWC, I sat down with Joe Constantine from @ericsson to unpack what it really takes to move from automation theater to real autonomous networks.

The answer surprised a lot of people.

Let’s dig in.

#MWC26 #EricssonMWC Ericsson Ambassador @EricssonNA

items.title:
Here is the cheapest transcription API in the world. Period.

Let's be honest:

Transcription APIs a...
items.content:
Here is the cheapest transcription API in the world. Period.

Let's be honest:

Transcription APIs are among the most overpriced components of voice AI infrastructure right now.

If you are building a voice agent or any sort of conversational AI, a huge chunk of your bill is just transcription.

Get more than a few users, and the cost no longer makes sense.

The Modulate team launched Velma Transcribe, and I think it's simply the cheapest API I've seen:

$0.13 per 1,000 minutes of audio transcription.

This is just ridiculous pricing, and every other API I've ever used is way more expensive than that.

If you are building voice applications, this is gold.

items.title:
First, let’s clarify something most people miss:

There isn’t just “AI in telecom.”

There’s:

→ AI ...
items.content:
First, let’s clarify something most people miss:

There isn’t just “AI in telecom.”

There’s:

→ AI in RAN
→ AI for RAN
→ AI on top of RAN

AI in RAN is where things get tangible.

We’re talking:

→ 20% improvement in uplink spectral efficiency
→ 14% energy savings with smarter algorithms
→ 5x better positioning accuracy

In this industry, 20% is massive. Spectrum is expensive. Energy is expensive. Latency is unforgiving.

This is not incremental. It’s structural.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon details AI in RAN performance improvements: 20% uplink spectral efficiency, 14% energy savings, 5x positioning accuracy.

items.content:
The real breakthrough is coordination.

These systems combine:

→ Precision measurement systems
→ High performance actuators
→ AI based motion control

This allows smooth, controlled movement in environments built for humans.

Which means one robot could potentially:

→ Assemble batteries
→ Move components
→ Assist different workstations

Flexibility becomes the new productivity multiplier.

items.title:
Traditional industrial robots are powerful, but limited.

They usually do one task
In one place
For ...
items.content:
Traditional industrial robots are powerful, but limited.

They usually do one task
In one place
For years

Humanoid robots change that equation.

Instead of fixed automation, they introduce mobile intelligence.

Key capability:

→ Multimodal sensor fusion
They process multiple types of sensory data simultaneously.

→ 3D spatial intelligence
They understand machines, spaces, and human movement.

In other words, they see the factory like a human would.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Thread continuation contrasting traditional fixed industrial robots with humanoid robots that use multimodal sensor fusion and 3D spatial intelligence for mobile factory work.
items.reasoning: Provides useful framing of the paradigm shift from fixed automation to mobile intelligence. Low engagement.

items.title:
But the real shift isn’t just better models.

It’s better data.

Joe Constantine made a point I full...
items.content:
But the real shift isn’t just better models.

It’s better data.

Joe Constantine made a point I fully agree with:

The winners in AI won’t be the companies with the best AI engineers.

They’ll be the ones with:

→ Real-time data
→ Trusted data
→ Direct access to network state

Today, most lifecycle management is event-based and delayed.

If you want to move from Quality of Service to Quality of Experience, you cannot operate on stale data.

Intent-based networks require streaming awareness.

Real time decisions.
Real time context.
Real time value.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon argues the real AI shift in telecom is about better data - real-time, trusted, direct access to network state - not just better models.

Here’s my big takeaway:

AI in telecom is no longer about experimentation.

It’s about collapsing the data layer, enabling intent-based control, and monetizing experience instead of raw connectivity.

If you are a CTO, CSP, or cloud leader, this is not optional strategy. It is survival architecture.

Watch the full conversation to understand where AI in, for, and on the RAN is heading next.

items.title:
So how do operators actually move toward Level 4 autonomy?

Two words: flatten architecture.

Histor...
items.content:
So how do operators actually move toward Level 4 autonomy?

Two words: flatten architecture.

Historically:

Every node had its own manager.
Every domain operated in isolation.
Topology visibility was fragmented.

The shift:

→ Unified SMO architecture
→ Standardized interfaces
→ rApps steering the network from above

This is where AI for RAN becomes agentic.
Applications that do not just analyze, but act.

That’s the first real step toward autonomy.

So how do operators actually move toward Level 4 autonomy?

Two words: flatten architecture.

Historically:

Every node had its own manager.

Every domain operated in isolation.

Topology visibility was fragmented.

The shift:

→ Unified SMO architecture

→ Standardized interfaces

→ rApps steering the network from above

This is where AI for RAN becomes agentic.

Applications that do not just analyze, but act.

That’s the first real step toward autonomy.

items.title: I once ran a panel at Brian Zisk's music and tech conference where the woman who ran Coachella, and ...
items.content:
I once ran a panel at Brian Zisk's music and tech conference where the woman who ran Coachella, and another who ran EDC in Vegas, and I talked about generational shifts in music that I noticed by going to several music festivals, including Coachella.

First saw Skrillex at Austin City Limits right before he got really popular. Was eating lunch on the hill, thanks to Dell who invited me, and saw one of the venues had 10,000 people jumping in unison. That was Skrillex. Ran down, joined them, and have been a fan ever since.

But the generational shifts in music came out of going to Coachella with @thomashawk and we were carrying big long lenses and shooting lots of photos. 

Kept noticing old people would drop their kids off at the Sahara Tent (which is where electronic music was being played). It was obvious that everyone in that tent was having way more fun than the other venues, but noticed that almost universally old people (my age) refused to go in, preferring the other venues that played rock or jazz. 

I started interviewing these old people and asked them why they weren't going where the fun was? 

Common answers:

1. They don't play instruments. Just push on a computer.
2. Their songs don't have lyrics, they wanted a story to come along with music.
3. They don't like the sound, just not familiar. 

Young people loved it, where the old people hated it. 

Today, about a decade later, these artists are the mainstream favorites and play on the big field. 

Lesson: if you want to know where music will be watch the teenagers. Duh, right?

But same is true in technology. 

Joining up with a 21-year-old (@blevlabs) was one of the best things I have done recently. He's an AI genius who built the AI system I'm using. I'm the first independent person to get to use it, all his other clients are big companies because his cognitive architecture is so good, but more expensive to run than, say, using Grok, ChatGPT, or Claude. 

We are getting close to launch. I can't wait to show you what we've built. 

And if you don't like Skrillex, well, stand in line behind my wife. :-)
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer tells long story about generational music shifts at Coachella, draws parallel to tech adoption, teases upcoming AI product built with 21-year-old developer using expensive cognitive architecture.
items.reasoning: Mostly personal storytelling with a minor AI product tease at the end. The AI product mention is too vague to be actionable.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer tells long story about generational music shifts at Coachella, draws parallel to tech adoption, teases upcoming AI product built with 21-year-old developer using expensive cognitive architecture.

items.content:
XGBoost for Regression, Predictive Modeling, and Time Series Analysis — Learn how to build, evaluate, & deploy predictive models:
https://t.co/DsGwnEX177 v/ @PacktDataML
—
My review:

🟠XGBoost is definitely the focal point and central contribution of this book, along with all those other things: Regression Trees, Predictive Modeling, Time Series Analysis, and Forecasting. 

🟠This book’s added value comes in its thorough progression from start to finish with comprehensive coverage of all the major steps and issues in data science and machine learning modeling: data cleaning, imbalanced data, feature engineering, feature engineering pipeline, feature importance, categorical data encoding, model interpretability and explainability, ensembles, model training, model evaluation metrics, hyperparameter tuning, model deployment, and more - including a quick-start case study starter project that feeds into next-level projects. 

🟠I highly recommend this broadly useful book for all levels of practitioners.

items.title: Scaling Pandas to Petabytes — The Architecture and Tradeoffs of BigQuery DataFrames: https://t.co/3E...
items.content:
Scaling Pandas to Petabytes — The Architecture and Tradeoffs of BigQuery DataFrames: https://t.co/3EtchWefBz by @odbmsorg 
⬇️

https://t.co/fmJ1boJWHo

BigQuery DataFrames (BigFrames) is an open-source Python library that provides a Pandas-compatible API for analyzing data stored in BigQuery. Unlike Pandas, which loads data into local memory, BigFrames translates operations into BigQuery SQL, enabling data scientists to work with terabyte-scale datasets using familiar Pandas syntax.

items.title: 💥Scikit-learn Cookbook — 80+ recipes for #MachineLearning in Python with scikit-learn [3rd Edition]:...
items.content:
💥Scikit-learn Cookbook — 80+ recipes for #MachineLearning in Python with scikit-learn [3rd Edition]: https://t.co/Z32D8jOzxC v/ @PacktDataML

𝓒𝓸𝓷𝓽𝓮𝓷𝓽𝓼:
🔹Common Conventions & API Elements of Scikit-Learn
🔹Pre-Model Workflow and Data Preprocessing
🔹Dimensionality Reduction Techniques
🔹Build Models with Distance Metrics & Nearest Neighbors
🔹Linear Models and Regularization
🔹Advanced Logistic Regression and Extensions
🔹Support Vector Machines and Kernel Methods
🔹Tree-Based Algorithms and Ensemble Methods
🔹Text Processing and Multiclass Classification
🔹Clustering Techniques
🔹Novelty and Outlier Detection
🔹Cross-Validation and Model Evaluation Techniques
🔹Deploying Scikit-Learn Models in Production

My Review (on Amazon):
I love this book, not only for its amazing content, but also for its brilliant adherence to its "Cookbook" label. The table of contents looks repetitive, but that's the magic of it. Find the algorithm, method, or application that you want "to cook up," and the presentation follows a uniform format from chapter to chapter. This is an extraordinarily practical, useful, and reader-friendly style, perfect for the target audience: anyone who needs to use (or is discovering how to use) specific Scikit-learn tools for their Python-based machine learning tasks. Consequently, this is great not only for individual use but also for training workshops and other educational settings. Python code in a cookbook context has never looked so good. IMHO.
